content.text: What is a Postmortem?

A postmortem (also called a post-incident review or blameless retrospective) is a structured analysis conducted after a notable system failure, quality degradation, or near-miss. For agentic AI systems the discipline has the same goal as in traditional site reliability engineering — understand what happened, why it happened, and how to prevent recurrence — but the failure modes are more varied: an agent can fail at the infrastructure layer (service down), the model layer (hallucinated tool call, reasoning loop), or the data/prompt layer (context corruption, prompt injection, eval gap). A good postmortem captures all three layers in a single document and closes the loop by tracking action items to completion.

The canonical postmortem document has five sections: a timeline, an impact summary, a root cause analysis (RCA), contributing factors, and action items with owners and due dates. For agent systems a sixth section is often useful: a trace excerpt showing the exact sequence of LLM calls, tool invocations, and intermediate state that preceded the failure.
Why It Matters

Agentic systems fail in ways that are difficult to reproduce from logs alone. A single production trace can involve dozens of LLM calls, branching tool selections, and dynamic memory reads. Without a disciplined postmortem process, the same failure class recurs because the team patches the symptom (e.g., raises a retry limit) without identifying the structural cause (e.g., the evaluator never checked that the tool schema matched the model's expectation). Organizations that skip postmortems also fail to accumulate institutional knowledge: the on-call engineer who triaged the incident leaves, and six months later another engineer re-discovers the same failure from scratch.

Postmortems also justify investment in observability. When a team can point to three postmortems that would have been resolved in minutes rather than hours with better tracing, it becomes straightforward to prioritize instrumentation work. The postmortem corpus is the paper trail that connects production pain to engineering investment.
Benefits
Prevents recurrence
: Action items target systemic causes rather than surface symptoms, so the same failure class is less likely to strike again.
Builds institutional memory

: A searchable postmortem database lets new engineers learn from past failures without repeating them. Filtering by failure category (prompt / model / tool / infra) surfaces patterns across incidents.
Improves observability
: Each postmortem reveals where a trace was missing or a metric was absent, driving a concrete backlog of observability improvements.
Expands eval coverage
: The specific input that caused the failure becomes a regression test, making the eval suite progressively more representative of production.
Fosters a blameless culture
: When engineers are not blamed for failures they are more willing to surface near-misses early, before they become outages.
Supports compliance
: Regulated industries (healthcare, finance) increasingly require written incident records for AI systems. Postmortems provide an audit trail.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Time cost
: A thorough postmortem for a complex agent incident can take 4–8 engineering hours to write and review, competing with feature work.
Action item debt
: Organizations that write postmortems without allocating capacity to execute action items accumulate a misleading record of 'resolved' incidents with open follow-ups.
Hindsight bias
: The root cause in a postmortem is always constructed in retrospect. The actual decision path at the time may have been reasonable given the information available.
LLM non-determinism
: Agent failures involving temperature >0 sampling may be difficult or impossible to reproduce exactly, limiting the ability to confirm that a fix works.
Scoping challenge

: Not every agent quality issue warrants a full postmortem. Calibrating which incidents meet the threshold requires explicit policy (severity rubric), otherwise either all incidents are reviewed (burnout) or none are (no learning).
How It Works

A postmortem follows a standard lifecycle. First, the incident is detected and mitigated; the priority is restoring service, not analysis. Once the system is stable, a lead is assigned to write the postmortem. The lead reconstructs the timeline from traces, logs, alerting events, and on-call notes. The timeline is the foundation: every statement of cause and effect must be anchored to a timestamp.

Root cause analysis (RCA) uses structured methods — most commonly the 5 Whys — to trace from the observed symptom back to the systemic weakness. For agent systems each 'Why' should explicitly name the layer: infra, model, tool schema, prompt, eval coverage, or deployment process. Contributing factors are conditions that made the failure more likely or more severe but are not the root cause itself. The distinction matters: root cause gets the primary action item, contributing factors get secondary ones.

Action items are the only output that prevents recurrence. Each item must have an owner (a specific person, not a team), a due date, and a link to the tracking ticket. The postmortem is not closed until all action items are either completed or explicitly deferred with justification. A follow-up review at 30 and 90 days checks whether the items actually closed the failure mode.
Architecture Overview

attrs.alt: Agent postmortem process: Incident → Detect/Mitigate → Reconstruct timeline → Root cause analysis → Contributing factors → Action items → Postmortem doc → Feedback loop → Verify fixes
attrs.title: Postmortem process flow for agentic AI systems

content.text: When to Use It
Not every agent glitch warrants a full postmortem. Define a severity rubric and trigger postmortems above a threshold. A common scheme for agentic systems:
P0 — full postmortem required
: The agent was completely unavailable for >5 minutes, or it took a destructive action in production (deleted records, sent messages, made purchases) without authorization.
P1 — postmortem required
: The agent was severely degraded (high error rate, significantly elevated latency) for >15 minutes, or a subset of users received materially wrong answers.
P2 — lightweight postmortem or ticket
: Quality regression (eval suite passing rate dropped significantly), latent bug discovered in review, or third-party model behavior change.
P3 — near-miss record
: An engineer caught a problem before it reached users. A short near-miss record (5 minutes of writing) is enough; the goal is to make the detection automatic next time.
How to Implement
Declare the incident and assign an incident commander
: One person owns communication and escalation. Everyone else focuses on mitigation. This separation prevents the 'too many cooks' anti-pattern.
Mitigate first
: Roll back the agent version, switch to a fallback model or a stub response, or disable the feature flag. Do not spend time diagnosing until users are unblocked.
Preserve evidence

: Export the relevant LLM traces, log spans, and metric snapshots before they expire from your observability retention window. For LLM systems this typically includes the full prompt/completion pairs, tool call arguments and responses, and token counts.
Build the timeline

: List every event with a UTC timestamp. Include: first alert time, on-call acknowledgment, diagnosis steps, each mitigation attempt, and resolution. For agentic failures annotate each LLM call node in the trace with its latency, token count, and the decision it produced.
Run the 5-Whys on the trace

: Start from the observed symptom. Ask 'Why?' and answer it with evidence from the trace. Repeat until you reach a system property (missing guard rail, absent eval, incorrect schema) that can be fixed, not just a specific bad output.
Write action items with SMART criteria

: Each action item should be Specific, Measurable, Assigned to one person, Realistic, and Time-bound. Bad example: 'Improve monitoring.' Good example: 'Add a Prometheus alert on agent_tool_error_rate > 0.05 for 2 minutes. Owner: @alice. Due: 2024-08-15. Ticket: #4421.'
Add a regression eval
: Convert the input that caused the failure into an eval test case. This ensures the next model update or prompt change is automatically checked against this scenario.
Hold the review meeting

: Circulate the draft 24 hours before the meeting. The meeting is for discussing open questions, not for reading the document aloud. Keep it to 30 minutes; the document is the artifact, not the conversation.
Track action items to closure

: Link each action item to a ticket. Set up a weekly review of open postmortem items so nothing lingers indefinitely. Verify that the fix actually prevents the failure, not just that the ticket was closed.
Examples
Postmortem data model and document writer

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum
import json


class Severity(str, Enum):
    P0 = "P0"  # full outage / destructive action
    P1 = "P1"  # severe degradation
    P2 = "P2"  # quality regression
    P3 = "P3"  # near-miss


class FailureLayer(str, Enum):
    INFRA = "infra"       # database, network, service unavailable
    MODEL = "model"       # LLM hallucination, reasoning loop, refusal
    TOOL = "tool"         # wrong schema, unexpected response, timeout
    PROMPT = "prompt"     # prompt drift, injection, context overflow
    DATA = "data"         # bad training data, retrieval quality
    HUMAN = "human"       # operator error, misconfiguration


@dataclass
class TimelineEvent:
    timestamp: datetime
    description: str
    actor: str  # system name or person


@dataclass
class ActionItem:
    summary: str
    owner: str
    due_date: str  # ISO date
    ticket_url: str
    completed: bool = False


@dataclass
class AgentPostmortem:
    title: str
    severity: Severity
    detected_at: datetime
    resolved_at: datetime
    author: str
    summary: str                        # 2-3 sentence executive summary
    timeline: list[TimelineEvent] = field(default_factory=list)
    root_cause: str = ""
    root_cause_layer: FailureLayer = FailureLayer.INFRA
    contributing_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    action_items: list[ActionItem] = field(default_factory=list)
    trace_excerpt: str = ""            # relevant LLM trace snippet
    regression_eval_added: bool = False

    @property
    def ttd(self) -> float:
        """Time-to-detect in minutes."""
        return (self.detected_at - self.detected_at).total_seconds() / 60

    @property
    def ttr(self) -> float:
        """Time-to-resolve in minutes."""
        return (self.resolved_at - self.detected_at).total_seconds() / 60

    def to_markdown(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"# Postmortem: {self.title}",
            f"**Severity**: {self.severity.value}  ",
            f"**Detected**: {self.detected_at.isoformat()}  ",
            f"**Resolved**: {self.resolved_at.isoformat()}  ",
            f"**TTR**: {self.ttr:.0f} min  ",
            f"**Author**: {self.author}",
            "",
            "## Summary",
            self.summary,
            "",
            "## Timeline",
        ]
        for ev in sorted(self.timeline, key=lambda e: e.timestamp):
            lines.append(f"- `{ev.timestamp.isoformat()}` **{ev.actor}**: {ev.description}")
        lines += [
            "",
            "## Root Cause",
            f"**Layer**: {self.root_cause_layer.value}",
            self.root_cause,
            "",
            "## Contributing Factors",
        ]
        for f_ in self.contributing_factors:
            lines.append(f"- {f_}")
        lines += ["", "## Action Items"]
        for i, item in enumerate(self.action_items, 1):
            status = "[x]" if item.completed else "[ ]"
            lines.append(
                f"{i}. {status} **{item.summary}** — @{item.owner} · due {item.due_date} · {item.ticket_url}"
            )
        if self.trace_excerpt:
            lines += ["", "## Trace Excerpt", "```", self.trace_excerpt, "```"]
        lines += ["", f"Regression eval added: {'yes' if self.regression_eval_added else 'NO — required action item'}"]
        return "\n".join(lines)
5-Whys analysis helper

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

SYSTEM = """
You are an expert site reliability engineer specialising in agentic AI systems.
Given a symptom and a trace excerpt from an LLM agent, perform a 5-Whys root
cause analysis. For each Why, cite evidence from the trace. Classify the root
cause layer as one of: infra, model, tool, prompt, data, human.
Output JSON with keys: whys (list of {why, evidence}), root_cause (str),
root_cause_layer (str), contributing_factors (list[str]).
"""


def run_5_whys(symptom: str, trace_excerpt: str) -> dict:
    """
    Ask Claude to perform a blameless 5-Whys on an agent trace.

    Returns a dict with keys: whys, root_cause, root_cause_layer,
    contributing_factors.
    """
    import json

    user_msg = (
        f"Symptom: {symptom}\n\n"
        f"Agent trace:\n{trace_excerpt}\n\n"
        "Perform a 5-Whys analysis. Output only the JSON."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text.strip()
    # Strip markdown code fences if model added them
    if raw.startswith("```"):
        raw = raw.split("\n", 1)[1].rsplit("```", 1)[0].strip()
    return json.loads(raw)


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    symptom = "Agent called delete_user_account with user_id=None, deleting 0 accounts but logging a success."
    trace = """
    [TURN 1] user: "Remove the test accounts I created last week."
    [LLM] Reasoning: I need to find test accounts first. Calling list_users with filter=test.
    [TOOL] list_users({"filter": "test"}) -> {"users": [], "total": 0}
    [LLM] Reasoning: No test users found. The task said 'last week' so perhaps they were
    already deleted. I'll call delete_user_account to confirm cleanup.
    [TOOL] delete_user_account({"user_id": None}) -> {"deleted": 0, "status": "ok"}
    [LLM] Task complete. All test accounts removed.
    """
    result = run_5_whys(symptom, trace)
    for i, why in enumerate(result["whys"], 1):
        print(f"Why {i}: {why['why']}")
        print(f"  Evidence: {why['evidence']}")
    print(f"\nRoot cause ({result['root_cause_layer']}): {result['root_cause']}")
    print("Contributing factors:")
    for cf in result["contributing_factors"]:
        print(f"  - {cf}")
Real-world Example

Consider a customer-service agent that routes support tickets and drafts reply emails. At 14:32 UTC on a Monday the on-call alert fires: the agent error rate spiked to 38% over 5 minutes. The on-call engineer checks traces and sees a pattern: every failed trace contains a tool call to the CRM with a malformed customer_id field — a string like 'CUS-00042' instead of the expected integer 42. The mitigation is to disable the CRM tool and fall back to a static response template.

The postmortem reveals that the CRM vendor silently changed their API response format over the weekend: the customer_id field changed from integer to string (prefixed). The agent's tool definition expected an integer, so when it read the CRM response and tried to pass customer_id back in a follow-up call, the value was now a string, causing a schema validation failure. The 5-Whys identifies the root cause as a missing contract test between the tool schema and the live CRM API. Contributing factors: no changelog monitoring on the CRM API, the eval suite used mock responses that still returned integers, and there was no canary deployment to catch schema drift before full rollout.
Contract test added as regression eval

import pytest
import requests


CRM_BASE = "https://api.crm.example.com/v2"
AGENT_TOOL_SCHEMA = {
    "customer_id": int,     # what the agent tool definition expects
    "name": str,
    "email": str,
}


def get_live_customer_schema() -> dict:
    """Hit the real CRM API and extract field types from a canary record."""
    resp = requests.get(f"{CRM_BASE}/customers/canary", timeout=5)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()
    return {field: type(value) for field, value in data.items()}


def test_crm_schema_matches_tool_definition():
    """
    Regression test added after postmortem PM-2024-08-12.
    Fails immediately when the CRM vendor changes a field type,
    so the drift is caught at CI time rather than in production.
    """
    live_schema = get_live_customer_schema()
    for field, expected_type in AGENT_TOOL_SCHEMA.items():
        assert field in live_schema, (
            f"CRM response missing expected field '{field}'. "
            "Update tool definition and re-run evals."
        )
        actual_type = live_schema[field]
        assert actual_type == expected_type, (
            f"Type mismatch for '{field}': tool expects {expected_type.__name__} "
            f"but CRM now returns {actual_type.__name__}. "
            f"See postmortem PM-2024-08-12 for context."
        )
Common Mistakes
Blaming the LLM

: Writing 'root cause: the model hallucinated' stops the analysis too early. The real question is: what system property allowed the hallucination to produce harm? Missing output validation, absent guardrails, or a prompt that invited guessing are the fixable causes.
Timeline without evidence
: A timeline that says 'engineer noticed something was wrong' without a timestamp or log reference cannot be verified. Every event needs a source: alert ID, trace ID, or log line.
Vague action items
: 'Improve prompt engineering' is not an action item. The postmortem writer must specify exactly what change is needed, who will make it, and by when.
Skipping the regression eval
: The input that caused the incident is the best possible new eval test case. Teams that fix the bug without adding the eval will rediscover the same failure after the next model update.
Treating every agent quality issue as a P0
: This creates postmortem fatigue. Calibrate severity thresholds carefully. P3 near-miss records should take under 10 minutes to write.
Losing evidence before writing
: LLM trace retention windows are often short (24–72 hours). If the postmortem draft is not started quickly the primary evidence may be gone, leaving only secondary indicators.
Best Practices
Blameless by design
: Name actions on systems, not people. 'The deployment pipeline did not enforce schema validation' instead of 'John skipped the validation step.'
Classify by failure layer
: Always tag the root cause with one of: infra, model, tool, prompt, data, human. Over time this taxonomy reveals which layer generates the most incidents, guiding investment priorities.
Extend trace retention on incident detection
: When an alert fires, immediately copy the relevant traces to cold storage. Do not rely on your observability platform's rolling retention window for forensics.
Separate detection time from occurrence time
: Agents can silently produce wrong outputs for hours before an alert fires. The timeline should include both when the problem started (from trace timestamps) and when it was detected (first alert).
Publish postmortems broadly
: A postmortem that is only read by the incident team provides one team's worth of learning. Published internally, it prevents other teams from building the same trap.
Quarterly postmortem themes review

: Once per quarter, scan the postmortem corpus for recurring failure classes. If 'tool schema mismatch' appears in three incidents, it is systemic — invest in a contract testing framework rather than fixing individual tool definitions.
Performance and Scalability
The postmortem process itself has performance characteristics to consider. For a high-volume agent deployment you may have dozens of P2/P3 incidents per month. Reduce the overhead by:
Templated postmortem documents
: A Google Doc or Notion template with the required sections pre-filled reduces write time from 2 hours to 45 minutes for experienced engineers.
Automated timeline reconstruction
: Write a script that queries your observability backend by incident time range and exports a chronological event list. This alone saves 30–60 minutes per postmortem.
AI-assisted draft

: Feed the trace, alert history, and on-call notes to an LLM to generate a draft timeline and initial 5-Whys. Engineers then verify and correct the draft. This does not remove human judgment; it removes the blank-page friction.
Action item tracking integration

: Integrate postmortem tooling with your issue tracker (Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues) so that action items become tickets automatically. Manual copy-paste from doc to tracker is a common point of loss.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Tool
Category
Strengths for agent postmortems
Backstage
Developer portal
Postmortem plugin; links incidents to service catalog entries for the agent and its dependencies
Incident.io
Incident management
Timeline auto-built from Slack thread; action items sync to Jira/Linear; AI draft postmortem feature
FireHydrant
Incident management
Retrospective module with template enforcement; integrates with PagerDuty and observability platforms
Langfuse
LLM observability
Full LLM trace retention; link trace IDs directly into postmortem timeline; replay traces for analysis
Honeycomb
Observability
Wide-event model captures arbitrary fields per LLM span; excellent for exploring agent trace data during RCA
Notion / Confluence
Knowledge base
Searchable postmortem corpus; tag by failure layer and severity; low friction for writing
Comparison with Alternatives
Practice
Scope
Strengths
Weaknesses
Postmortem
Post-incident
Deep root cause; institutional memory; drives evals and observability investment
Time cost; hindsight bias; requires evidence preservation discipline
Alert + auto-remediation only
Real-time
Fast MTTR; no manual effort
Masks root causes; same failure recurs; no learning
Sprint retrospective

Broad team health review; surfacing friction and slow issues
Too infrequent for live incidents; lacks technical depth for agent failures
Chaos engineering
Pre-incident
Proactively discovers failure modes before production
Cannot simulate novel LLM behaviors; complements but does not replace postmortems
Security and Reliability

Postmortem documents themselves carry security risk. They contain full prompt/completion traces, which may include PII (names, emails, account IDs from production traffic) and proprietary system prompts. Treat postmortem documents with the same access controls as the production data they describe. Store them in a system that enforces role-based access, not a public wiki or an open Slack channel.

For incidents involving potential security compromise — prompt injection that exfiltrated data, a tool that accessed unauthorized resources — the postmortem should be flagged and routed through a security review before broader distribution. Redact PII from trace excerpts before publishing to a team-wide knowledge base.

On the reliability side, the postmortem process itself needs reliability: a rigid 5-business-day SLA enforces that the analysis is done while evidence and memory are fresh. Beyond that window, human recollection degrades and traces may have expired. Organizations should monitor the percentage of P0/P1 incidents that receive a postmortem within the SLA and treat a declining rate as a leading indicator of organizational reliability debt.
Key Takeaways

A postmortem is a structured written analysis of an incident that traces from symptom to root cause and produces action items that prevent recurrence; it is the primary mechanism for organizational learning from agent failures.
Agent incidents require an extended failure taxonomy beyond infra: model hallucination, tool schema mismatch, prompt drift, context overflow, and eval gap are all first-class failure categories.
Mitigate first, analyze second; preserve LLM traces immediately on incident declaration because retention windows are short.
The 5-Whys applied to an agent trace produces a root cause that is actionable (a system property to fix) rather than attributing the failure to an opaque model behavior.
Every postmortem should produce at least one new eval test case from the failing input; this is the compounding return that makes the eval suite progressively more production-representative.
Action items must have a named owner, a due date, and a tracking ticket; vague team-level assignments do not get completed.

Blameless framing is not merely cultural — it is operationally necessary for agentic systems where the 'failure' is often a combination of human prompt design, model inference, and tool integration, not a single person's error.
Postmortem documents contain sensitive production data; apply access controls and redact PII from trace excerpts before broader distribution.
